title: useState and snapshots

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for useState and snapshots; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to useState and snapshots
sources.claim: useState stores component state between renders and its setter queues a replacement or updater for a later render.

canonicalExplanation.summary: useState stores component state between renders and its setter queues a replacement or updater for a later render.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Three increments use functional updates so each derives from the latest queued count. Your goal is to trace and implement state updates without stale arithmetic or direct mutation. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Props and composition; javascript events model; Render purity and Strict Mode; javascript scope closures. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: useState stores component state between renders and its setter queues a replacement or updater for a later render. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Updates batch, stale closures see prior snapshots, objects need replacement, and initializers can run more than once in development. This draft therefore rejects: Calling setCount(count+1) three times always increases count by three.

includes: Treat render state as a snapshot and use updater functions for queued changes based on prior state.
Trace and implement state updates without stale arithmetic or direct mutation.
excludes: Updates batch, stale closures see prior snapshots, objects need replacement, and initializers can run more than once in development.

outcomes: Trace and implement state updates without stale arithmetic or direct mutation.

examples.title: useState and snapshots: concrete case
examples.situation: Three increments use functional updates so each derives from the latest queued count.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Three increments use functional updates so each derives from the latest queued count.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: When next state depends on previous state, pass a functional updater to avoid stale snapshot calculations.
examples.result: The result follows because When next state depends on previous state, pass a functional updater to avoid stale snapshot calculations.
